turns.content: I'm onboarding to this new Rust codebase and I've seen some tests written with tables. What's the difference between table-driven tests and normal parameterized tests?

A good way to think about it is that a standard parameterized test creates many separate test functions, one for each input, while a table-driven approach uses a single test function that iterates over a Vec of tuples containing the inputs and expected outputs. This lets you declare all your test cases in one place using a Vec<(Input, Expected)> instead of repeating boilerplate code.
So I should just write separate functions for each case? That sounds easier than managing a big collection of tuples.

That's actually the opposite. Writing separate test functions leads to duplication and makes it hard to add new cases later without touching many files. With table-driven tests, you only write one function and update the Vec once when adding inputs or expected values.
